Author: Adnan Masood; PhD
Title: The Entry‑Level Squeeze — Why AI’s earliest impacts hit junior talent and what leaders can do…
Text:
Member-only story
The Entry‑Level Squeeze — Why AI’s earliest impacts hit junior talent and what leaders can do about it.
Inside the first employment shifts from generative AI — and how to respond.
“Canaries in the Coal Mine? Six Facts about the Recent Employment Effects of Artificial Intelligence” is an working paper from the Stanford Digital Economy Lab (HAI) authored by Erik Brynjolfsson, Bharat Chandar, and Ruyu Chen. It is one of the first studies to use high-frequency administrative payroll data from the largest U.S. payroll processor to track AI’s labor-market effects in near real time.
The paper combines this dataset — millions of workers across thousands of firms — with two independent occupational AI-exposure measures:
(1) GPT-4-based exposure estimates and
(2) Anthropic’s large-scale model usage data distinguishing automation vs. augmentation patterns.
Methodologically, the study relies on within-firm event-study models with firm-time fixed effects to isolate shifts in hiring from broader economic shocks. The paper is structured around “six facts” that describe observed employment patterns rather than theoretical predictions, and it is…
